docs.text: 📘 Day 1: Internship Orientation & Introduction to Python Programming 🧭 Overview The first day of the internship focused on orientation and foundational knowledge. We were introduced to the structure of the internship, expectations, rules, and the tools we will be using throughout the program. 📋 Internship Introduction During this session, we gained a clear understanding of: The objectives of the internship program Expected conduct and professional behavior Rules and regulations to be followed Evaluation criteria and performance expectations This helped set a strong foundation for a productive and disciplined internship experience. 🐍 Introduction to Python Programming We were also introduced to Python , one of the most widely used programming languages in the world. docs.text: 🔌 Day 2: Introduction to Tinkercad & First Circuit Design Simulation 🧭 Overview On the second day, we explored practical skills by working with Tinkercad , an online simulation platform used for designing and testing electronic circuits. 🛠 Introduction to Tinkercad We were introduced to the features and capabilities of Tinkercad, including: Creating and managing projects Using electronic components (LEDs, resistors, Arduino, etc.) Building circuits in a virtual environment Running simulations in real time ⚡ First Circuit Design We designed and simulated our first electronic circuit. Activities performed: Connecting basic components such as LEDs and resistors Using Arduino for control Writing and uploading code Running simulation to test functionality 💡 Sample Arduino Code // Simple LED Blink Program int led = 13; void setup() { pinMode(led, OUTPUT); } void loop() { digitalWrite(led, HIGH); delay(1000); digitalWrite(led, LOW); delay(1000); } 🖼 First Circuit Design Preview Below is the first circuit we designed and simulated using Tinkercad:

docs.text: Day 3 Automatic Gate System with Keypad Date: 01.04.2026 Description of Today's Activities: Today, we worked on designing and implementing an automatic gate system using a keypad interface . We focused on both the hardware and simulation aspects of the project: Keypad-controlled Gate: Programmed a keypad to control the opening and closing of the gate. Implemented password verification to allow authorized access only. Simulation in Tinkercad: Designed the gate system virtually using Tinkercad . Tested how the servo motor responds to correct and incorrect passwords. Verified the logic and movement of the gate before actual implementation. This activity helped us understand the integration of microcontrollers, input devices, and actuators , as well as the importance of simulation before physical deployment . Image of the Design:
docs.title: Day 3

docs.text: Day 4: RFID-Based Server Room Access Control System with Role Identification 📝 Description On Day 4 of the internship, we designed and implemented a complete RFID-based access control system for a server room. The system uses the RFID RC522 module to scan access cards assigned to different roles, including Manager, Technical Manager, and Assistant. Each card is uniquely identified using its UID, and when scanned, the system verifies whether the card is authorized. If access is granted, the LCD displays the name of the user, the servo motor unlocks the door, and the buzzer produces a single beep as confirmation. If the card is not recognized, access is denied, and the buzzer sounds three times to indicate rejection. This project helped us understand real-world security systems, role-based authentication, and the integration of multiple components such as RFID, LCD, servo motors, and buzzers in embedded systems. 🛠️ Components Used Arduino Uno RFID RC522 Module RFID Cards (3) LCD 16x2 (I2C) Servo Motor Buzzer Jumper Wires ⚙️ System Functionality Displays user identity on LCD Grants access to authorized users Denies access to unknown users Uses servo motor as door lock Provides buzzer feedback for actions 📸 Project Image
docs.title: Day 4: RFID-Based Server Room Access Control System with Role Identification
Day 4: RFID-Based Server Room Access Control System with Role Identification

docs.text: On Day 4 of the internship, we designed and implemented a complete RFID-based access control system for a server room. The system uses the RFID RC522 module to scan access cards assigned to different roles, including Manager, Technical Manager, and Assistant. Each card is uniquely identified using its UID, and when scanned, the system verifies whether the card is authorized. If access is granted, the LCD displays the name of the user, the servo motor unlocks the door, and the buzzer produces a single beep as confirmation. If the card is not recognized, access is denied, and the buzzer sounds three times to indicate rejection. This project helped us understand real-world security systems, role-based authentication, and the integration of multiple components such as RFID, LCD, servo motors, and buzzers in embedded systems.

docs.text: Day 5: Smart Room Automation & Real-Time Environmental Monitoring System 📝 Description On Day 5 of the internship, we designed and conceptualized an advanced smart room automation system integrated with real-time environmental monitoring using the ESP32 microcontroller and the Blynk IoT platform. The system was developed to simulate intelligent control of multiple electrical appliances while simultaneously tracking key environmental parameters. Four LEDs were utilized to represent controlled devices within the room: the first and second LEDs simulated two independent fans, while the third and fourth LEDs represented two separate lighting units. This multi-device setup demonstrated how modern smart systems can manage multiple loads efficiently and independently. In addition to automation, the system incorporated environmental sensing capabilities. The Blynk IoT dashboard was configured to display real-time data, including temperature, humidity, and soil moisture levels. These metrics are essential for maintaining optimal environmental conditions and are widely applied in smart homes, server rooms, and agricultural monitoring systems. The integration of control and monitoring into a single IoT platform provided practical insight into how embedded systems communicate with cloud-based applications. This project emphasized scalability, remote accessibility, and the importance of user-friendly interfaces in modern automation systems. By the end of the session, we strengthened our understanding of IoT architecture, real-time data visualization, and multi-device control, all of which are fundamental in building efficient and intelligent smart environments. 🛠️ Components Used ESP32 Microcontroller DHT Sensor (Temperature & Humidity) Soil Moisture Sensor LEDs (4) – representing fans and lamps Resistors Jumper Wires WiFi Connectivity Blynk IoT Platform ⚙️ System Functionality Controls multiple devices independently (fans and lamps) Displays real-time temperature readings Displays real-time humidity levels Monitors soil moisture conditions Provides remote control via mobile application Enables real-time data visualization on IoT dashboard Demonstrates integration of automation and environmental monitoring 📸 Project Image
docs.title: Day 5

docs.text: Week 2 - Day 1: Introduction to PHP & Web Dashboard Description On the first day of Week 2, we were introduced to PHP , a server-side scripting language for web development. We focused on creating a web dashboard for our DHT sensor project to monitor temperature and humidity. Objectives Learn basics of PHP for web development Set up a local development environment (XAMPP) Retrieve sensor data from Arduino using PHP Build a web dashboard interface Implement simple admin login functionality Understand data flow from IoT sensors to the web Key Activities Installed and configured XAMPP for local PHP development Created PHP scripts to fetch DHT sensor data Designed a dashboard interface using HTML, CSS, and PHP Tested real-time display of temperature and humidity Implemented admin login to secure dashboard access Outcome By the end of the day, we had a working web dashboard displaying live sensor data, ready for monitoring temperature and humidity remotely.
docs.title: Week 2 - Day 1: Introduction to PHP & Web Dashboard
Week 2 - Day 1: Introduction to PHP & Web Dashboard

docs.text: Day 3: IoT Data Communication and PHP Dashboard Integration 📝 Description On Day 3, we moved from simulation to real-world system integration by connecting our IoT project to a web-based dashboard. We learned how sensor data (temperature and humidity) can be transmitted from a microcontroller (such as ESP8266) to a web server using HTTP requests. We created a MySQL database to store incoming sensor data and developed a PHP script to receive and insert this data into the database. This allowed us to build the foundation of a real-time monitoring system. Additionally, we implemented a web dashboard using PHP that retrieves and displays the stored data, including temperature, humidity, and system status (ONLINE/OFFLINE). 🧪 What We Learned Sending data from IoT devices to a server Basics of PHP backend development Using MySQL databases Real-time data storage and retrieval Building a dynamic web dashboard 🧰 Technologies Used Arduino / ESP8266 PHP MySQL HTML & CSS XAMPP 📡 Sample Code (Data Receiver) ```php query($sql); ?>

docs.text: 📅 Day 4: RFID-Based Smart Room Access Dashboard 📝 Overview On Day 4 of the internship, we focused on building a complete RFID-based web dashboard system . The goal was to allow users to scan RFID cards and have their access data displayed in real-time on a web interface. This system integrates hardware (RFID scanner) with a web-based dashboard , enabling monitoring and logging of access activities. 🎯 Objectives Develop a web-based dashboard using PHP and MySQL Store RFID scan data in a database Display real-time logs of users accessing the system Implement user registration for RFID cards 🛠️ Technologies Used PHP – Backend logic MySQL – Database management HTML/CSS (Bootstrap) – User Interface XAMPP – Local server environment RFID RC522 Module – Card scanning ⚙️ System Workflow User taps RFID card UID is sent to the server ( scan.php ) Server stores UID in database Dashboard retrieves and displays logs Admin monitors access in real-time 💻 Key Features 🔐 RFID-based authentication 📊 Live dashboard display 🧾 Access logs with timestamps 👤 User registration system 🔄 Auto-refresh for real-time updates 🧩 Code Highlights 📌 Database Connection ( db.php ) ```php $conn = new mysqli("localhost", "root", "", "smart_room");
docs.title: 📅 Day 4: RFID-Based Smart Room Access Dashboard

docs.text: 📅 Day 5: ESP32 RFID Integration with Web Dashboard 📝 Overview On Day 5, we transitioned from simulation and web-based development to a real-world IoT implementation . We integrated an ESP32 microcontroller with an RFID system to create a fully functional smart access solution. This system enables real-time communication between hardware and a web server, allowing access data to be captured and displayed instantly on a dashboard. 🎯 Objectives Integrate ESP32 with an RFID system Establish wireless communication with the server Transmit RFID data to the web application Display real-time access logs on the dashboard Provide physical feedback using output components 🧰 Components Used ESP32 Development Board RFID Module RFID Cards/Tags Buzzer LED Jumper Wires Breadboard ⚙️ System Workflow An RFID card is scanned The microcontroller reads the card's unique identifier The device connects to a WiFi network The scanned data is transmitted to the server The server processes and stores the data The dashboard updates automatically Feedback is provided through visual and audio indicators 💻 System Features 📡 Wireless data transmission 🔐 RFID-based identification 📊 Real-time dashboard updates 🔔 Audio and visual feedback 🌐 Integration with web technologies 🚧 Challenges Faced Difficulty in establishing stable WiFi connection Incorrect configuration leading to failed communication Hardware wiring issues during setup Delays in real-time data transmission ✅ Solutions Implemented Ensured correct network configuration Verified system setup and parameters Carefully reviewed hardware connections Optimized communication flow for better performance 📚 Lessons Learned Practical integration of hardware and software systems Importance of proper configuration in IoT systems Troubleshooting real-world implementation issues Understanding real-time data communication 🚀 Conclusion Day 5 marked a significant milestone in the internship by transforming the project into a fully functional real-world IoT system . The successful integration of hardware with a web-based platform demonstrated the practical application of smart technologies in access control systems.

docs.text: Day 1 – Week 3 Designing and Configuring a Multi-Department Network in Cisco Packet Tracer 📝 Description On Day 1 of Week 3, we focused on designing and configuring a complete network infrastructure for a simulated company (S Ltd) using Cisco Packet Tracer. The company consisted of three departments: Administration, Sales, and IT, distributed across two floors within a single building. The objective was to ensure efficient communication, proper network segmentation, and shared resource access across all departments. We began by designing the physical topology, placing network devices such as routers, switches, end devices (PCs), a server, and a network printer. Each department was logically grouped and connected through switches based on their floor location. To enhance network organization and security, Virtual Local Area Networks (VLANs) were implemented. Each department was assigned a unique VLAN: VLAN 10 for Administration VLAN 20 for Sales VLAN 30 for IT This allowed traffic isolation while maintaining controlled inter-department communication. We configured trunk links between switches and the router to enable VLAN tagging and implemented Inter-VLAN routing using the Router-on-a-Stick method. This allowed devices from different VLANs to communicate through the router. Dynamic IP addressing was configured using DHCP on the router, ensuring automatic IP assignment to all client devices. Additionally, the server was configured with a static IP address and enabled with services such as HTTP and file sharing, allowing centralized resource access. The network printer was also configured and made accessible across all departments. Finally, connectivity testing was performed using ping commands to verify successful communication between devices, departments, and shared resources. 🎯 Objectives Achieved Designed a structured enterprise network Implemented VLAN segmentation Configured inter-VLAN routing Enabled DHCP for automatic IP assignment Set up a server for file sharing Configured a shared network printer Verified full network connectivity 🧰 Tools Used Cisco Packet Tracer VLANs Routing DHCP
docs.title: Day 1 – Week 3
Designing and Configuring a Multi-Department Network in Cisco Packet Tracer

docs.text: On Day 1 of Week 3, we focused on designing and configuring a complete network infrastructure for a simulated company (S Ltd) using Cisco Packet Tracer. The company consisted of three departments: Administration, Sales, and IT, distributed across two floors within a single building. The objective was to ensure efficient communication, proper network segmentation, and shared resource access across all departments. We began by designing the physical topology, placing network devices such as routers, switches, end devices (PCs), a server, and a network printer. Each department was logically grouped and connected through switches based on their floor location. To enhance network organization and security, Virtual Local Area Networks (VLANs) were implemented. Each department was assigned a unique VLAN: VLAN 10 for Administration VLAN 20 for Sales VLAN 30 for IT This allowed traffic isolation while maintaining controlled inter-department communication. We configured trunk links between switches and the router to enable VLAN tagging and implemented Inter-VLAN routing using the Router-on-a-Stick method. This allowed devices from different VLANs to communicate through the router. Dynamic IP addressing was configured using DHCP on the router, ensuring automatic IP assignment to all client devices. Additionally, the server was configured with a static IP address and enabled with services such as HTTP and file sharing, allowing centralized resource access. The network printer was also configured and made accessible across all departments. Finally, connectivity testing was performed using ping commands to verify successful communication between devices, departments, and shared resources.
